skill.name: python-packaging
skill.description: Create distributable Python packages with proper project structure, setup.py/pyproject.toml, and publishing to PyPI.
skill.summary: Comprehensive guide for building, structuring, and publishing Python packages using modern packaging standards and tools.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill consists entirely of markdown documentation teaching Python packaging best practices. Static analyzer flagged 118 patterns (backticks, URLs, config examples) that are documentation content, not executable code. All findings are false positives from code examples in fenced markdown blocks. No actual security risks detected.

medium_findings.title: Documentation flagged as code execution
medium_findings.description: Static analyzer detected backtick characters in markdown documentation. These are fenced code block delimiters in documentation, not actual shell execution. The skill contains no executable Python or shell code - only educational content about Python packaging practices.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs flagged as network activity
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected hardcoded URLs in documentation. These are reference links to PyPI, GitHub, and Python packaging resources - not actual network requests in code.

low_findings.title: Configuration examples flagged as sensitive file access
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected .pypirc configuration file references. This is documentation teaching users how to configure PyPI authentication - not unauthorized file access.

content.user_title: Build and publish Python packages with PyPI
content.value_statement: Python developers struggle with complex packaging workflows and inconsistent project structures. This skill provides complete guidance for creating professional Python packages using modern standards like pyproject.toml and setuptools.
actual_capabilities: Guide creation of Python package structure with src layout or flat layout
Generate pyproject.toml configurations with complete metadata
Create CLI tools using Click or argparse with entry points
Build wheels and source distributions for distribution
Publish packages to PyPI, TestPyPI, or private repositories
Configure automated publishing with GitHub Actions workflows
limitations: Provides documentation and guidance only - does not execute packaging commands
Requires user to have Python and packaging tools installed locally
Does not manage actual PyPI credentials or authentication tokens
Cannot validate package publishing success without user feedback

use_cases.title: Library Author Publishing
use_cases.description: Create a new Python library for distribution on PyPI with proper project structure, metadata, and automated publishing workflows.

use_cases.title: CLI Tool Distribution
use_cases.description: Package command-line tools with entry points, making them easily installable via pip with executable commands.
use_cases.target_user: Developers creating developer tools or utilities

use_cases.title: Enterprise Package Management
use_cases.description: Set up private package indexes and namespace packages for organizing code across multiple teams and repositories.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering teams managing internal Python packages

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic Python package structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new Python package called 'my-tool' with a src layout, pyproject.toml using setuptools, and a basic module structure. I want to include pytest for testing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setting up their first distributable package

prompt_templates.title: Add CLI entry points to existing package
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an existing Python package with utility functions. Show me how to add CLI entry points using Click so users can run 'mytool' from the command line after installation.

prompt_templates.title: Configure automated PyPI publishing
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that builds and publishes my Python package to PyPI when I create a new release. Include package validation and TestPyPI testing steps.

prompt_templates.title: Set up namespace packages for monorepo
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to split my company's codebase into multiple packages (company-core, company-api, company-utils) that share a namespace. Show me the complete project structure and pyproject.toml configurations.

output_examples.input: Create a pyproject.toml for a package named 'data-utils' with Click and pandas dependencies, pytest for dev, and a CLI entry point
output_examples.output: A complete pyproject.toml with build-system configuration, project metadata, dependencies with version constraints, optional dev dependencies, CLI script entry points, and tool configurations for black, ruff, and pytest.

output_examples.input: How do I publish my package to TestPyPI first?
output_examples.output: Step-by-step instructions: (1) Install twine, (2) Build package with python -m build, (3) Validate with twine check, (4) Upload to TestPyPI with twine upload --repository testpypi, (5) Test installation from TestPyPI, (6) Publish to production PyPI when ready.

best_practices: Use src/ layout to prevent accidental imports from uninstalled source directory
Define all metadata in pyproject.toml following PEP 621 for modern packaging
Test package installation in a clean virtual environment before publishing to PyPI
anti_patterns: Do not use setup.py as primary configuration - migrate to pyproject.toml
Avoid flat layout for libraries as it allows importing without installation
Never commit API tokens or credentials - use environment variables for PyPI authentication

faq.question: What is the difference between pyproject.toml and setup.py?
faq.answer: pyproject.toml is the modern standard (PEP 518/621) that declares build requirements and metadata in a single file. setup.py is the legacy approach using executable Python code. New projects should use pyproject.toml with a build backend like setuptools or hatchling.

faq.question: Should I publish to TestPyPI before PyPI?
faq.answer: Yes, always test on TestPyPI first. It is a separate index that mirrors PyPI's functionality. Upload your package there, install it in a clean environment, verify all functionality works, then publish to the real PyPI.

faq.question: How do I include data files in my package?
faq.answer: Use the package-data configuration in pyproject.toml under [tool.setuptools.package-data]. Specify patterns like '*.json' or 'templates/*.html'. Access files at runtime using importlib.resources.files() for modern Python.

faq.question: What version should I start with for my first release?
faq.answer: Start with 0.1.0 for initial releases to indicate the package is in early development. Use semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH). Move to 1.0.0 when you have a stable public API you do not want to break.

faq.question: Can I automatically version my package from git tags?
faq.answer: Yes, use setuptools-scm in your build-system.requires. Configure it in pyproject.toml to read version information from git tags. This ensures version matches git history and automates version management.

faq.question: How do I create a CLI tool that users can install with pip?
faq.answer: Define entry points in pyproject.toml under [project.scripts]. Map a command name to a Python function path like 'mytool = my_package.cli:main'. After installation, users can run 'mytool' from their terminal.
